prompt: Given a positive floating point number, it can be decomposed into
and integer part (largest integer smaller than given number) and decimals
Return the decimal part of the number.
response: Given a positive floating point number, it can be decomposed into
and integer part (largest integer smaller than given number) and decimals
Return the decimal part of the number.
The % (modulo) operator returns the remainder after division. When you do number % 1, it divides the number by 1 and returns the remainder — which is exactly the decimal part.
Alternatively, you could use number - Math.floor(number), which explicitly subtracts the integer part — both approaches yield the same result.
interactions.notes: Code used without modification. Claude used modulo operator (% 1) with integer instead of double. Also provided alternative approach using Math.floor().

prompt: The current base test for truncateNumber function has the following test smell detected by JNose:
- Magic Number Test (3 occurrences): Tests use unexplained numeric literals like 3.5, 0.5, 1.33, 0.33, 123.456, 0.456
Write improved JUnit test cases that:
1. Fix Magic Number smell by using named constants with descriptive comments explaining what each value represents
2. Add clear documentation for why these specific test values were chosen
response: Here are the improved JUnit test cases with all Magic Number smells resolved:
private final solutions.claude.Task2 s = new solutions.claude.Task2();
// Floating-point comparison tolerance: accounts for IEEE 754 rounding
// errors that arise when subtracting the integer part from a double
// Input: 3.5 — chosen because it has an exact binary representation,
// making the expected decimal part (.5) completely unambiguous
// Expected: 0.5 — the integer part (3) is stripped, leaving exactly 0.5
// Input: 1.33 — chosen to verify behaviour with a value whose decimal
// part cannot be represented exactly in binary floating point (1/3 ≈ 0.333…)
// Expected: 0.33 — integer part (1) stripped; tolerance covers the
// unavoidable IEEE 754 rounding error in the subtraction
// Input: 123.456 — chosen to stress-test with a large integer part and
// a multi-digit decimal that is not a simple power-of-two fraction
// Expected: 0.456 — only the fractional part survives after truncation
// ── Test case 4: just above a whole number ────────────────────────────
// Input: 5.0000001 — chosen to verify that a near-integer value still
// yields a non-zero decimal part and is not incorrectly rounded to 0
// Expected: 0.0000001 — the tiny fractional remainder must be preserved
// Input: 7.0 — chosen to verify that a number with no fractional part
// returns exactly 0.0 (the modulo of a whole number is always zero)
// Expected: 0.0 — no decimal part exists; result must be exactly zero
@DisplayName("3.5 → decimal part is 0.5 (exact binary representation, unambiguous)")
"3.5 has an exact binary form; decimal part must be exactly 0.5");
@DisplayName("1.33 → decimal part is ~0.33 (repeating binary, tolerance required)")
"1.33 cannot be represented exactly in binary; result should be ~0.33");
@DisplayName("123.456 → decimal part is ~0.456 (large integer part, multi-digit fraction)")
"123.456 should strip the large integer part 123, leaving ~0.456");
@DisplayName("5.0000001 → decimal part is ~0.0000001 (near-whole number is not rounded to zero)")
"A value just above a whole number must preserve the tiny fractional remainder");
@DisplayName("7.0 → decimal part is 0.0 (exact whole number has no fractional part)")
"An exact whole number has no decimal part; result must be 0.0");
How each Magic Number smell is eliminated:
| Raw literal | Named constant | Explanation provided |
| 3.5 | INPUT_SIMPLE_HALF | Exact binary representation — unambiguous result |
| 0.5 | EXPECTED_SIMPLE_HALF | Integer part (3) stripped, leaving exactly 0.5 |
| 1.33 | INPUT_REPEATING_DECIMAL | Cannot be represented exactly in binary (1/3 ≈ 0.333…) |
| 0.33 | EXPECTED_REPEATING_DECIMAL | IEEE 754 rounding error covered by tolerance |
| 123.456 | INPUT_MANY_DECIMALS | Large integer part + multi-digit fraction stress test |
| 0.456 | EXPECTED_MANY_DECIMALS | Only the fractional part survives truncation |
| 1e-6 | FLOATING_POINT_TOLERANCE | Accounts for IEEE 754 rounding in double subtraction |
interactions.notes: Claude provided comprehensive tests with named constants eliminating all Magic Number smells. Added extra test cases for near-whole and exact-whole numbers.
